title: Virtual destructor ensures proper cleanup
prompt_md:
## Goal

When you delete a derived object through a base-class pointer, the derived destructor only runs if the base destructor is virtual. Build a two-class hierarchy that proves this: deleting a derived object through a `Base*` must run BOTH destructors, in the correct order.

## Classes

- `Entity` is the base class. Its destructor prints `~Entity`.
- `Player` publicly derives from `Entity`, is built from the player's name, and its destructor prints `~Player(<name>)` where `<name>` is that name.

## Input

- Line 1: integer `N` (1 <= N <= 5).
- Next `N` lines: each a single-word name.

## Output

For each name in order: create a `Player` with that name on the heap, hold it through an `Entity*`, and delete it through that base pointer. Each deletion produces two lines, derived destructor first, then base:
```
~Player(<name>)
~Entity
```

For input `2` / `Alice` / `Bob`:
```
~Player(Alice)
~Entity
~Player(Bob)
~Entity
```

title: Resource-owning hierarchy with virtual destructor
prompt_md:
## Goal

Without a virtual destructor, deleting a derived object through a base pointer skips the derived destructor and leaks whatever it owns. Build a small shape hierarchy whose objects all destroy cleanly when deleted through base pointers.

## Classes

- `Shape` is the base class; its destructor prints `~Shape`.
- `Circle` derives from `Shape`, is built from an integer radius `r`, and its destructor prints `~Circle r=<r>`.
- `Square` derives from `Shape`, is built from an integer side `s`, and its destructor prints `~Square s=<s>`.

## Input

- Line 1: integer `N` (1 <= N <= 6).
- Next `N` lines: either `circle <r>` or `square <s>`.

## Output

Allocate every shape on the heap, holding each through a `Shape*`. After reading all of them, delete them in the order they were created. Each deletion prints the derived destructor line followed by `~Shape`.

For input `2` / `circle 5` / `square 3`:
```
~Circle r=5
~Shape
~Square s=3
~Shape
```
